authorship_evidence: Project created in 2020 by erev0s, pre-LLM era, consistent human commit history, referenced in multiple security tool evaluations

evidence.description: SQL injection via f-string interpolation of unsanitized username path parameter into raw SQL query: user_query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '{username}'" followed by db.session.execute(text(user_query)). Reachable from GET /users/v1/{username} without authentication.

Unauthorized password change (IDOR). When vuln=1, the update_password function uses the username from the URL path parameter to look up the user whose password to change, instead of using the authenticated user from the JWT token (resp['sub']). Any authenticated user can change any other user's password by sending PUT /users/v1/{victim_username}/password.

Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA). When vuln=1, get_by_title queries the book by title only (Book.query.filter_by(book_title=str(book_title)).first()) without verifying the authenticated user is the owner. Any authenticated user can read any book's secret_content by sending GET /books/v1/{book_title}.

Mass assignment vulnerability allowing privilege escalation. When vuln=1, the register_user function checks if 'admin' is present in the user-supplied JSON body and uses it to set the admin flag on the new user. An attacker can register as admin by including '"admin": true' in the POST /users/v1/register request body.

Excessive data exposure through unauthenticated debug endpoint. GET /users/v1/_debug returns all user details including plaintext passwords, email addresses, and admin status for every user via User.get_all_users_debug(). No authentication or authorization is required.

User and password enumeration via differential error messages. When vuln=1, login_user returns 'Password is not correct for the given username.' for valid usernames with wrong passwords (line 103) and 'Username does not exist' for invalid usernames (line 106). This allows attackers to enumerate valid usernames.

Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS). When vuln=1, update_email uses a complex regex with nested quantifiers: r"^([0-9a-zA-Z]([-.\.\w]*[0-9a-zA-Z])*@{1}([0-9a-zA-Z][-\w]*[0-9a-zA-Z]\.)+[a-zA-Z]{2,9})$". The nested groups ([-.\.\w]*[0-9a-zA-Z])* cause catastrophic backtracking with crafted input, consuming excessive CPU.

attack_chain: POST /users/v1/login — authenticate and obtain JWT token
PUT /users/v1/{username}/email with body {"email": "aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!"} — triggers catastrophic backtracking
poc.notes: Execution time doubles per additional character. 30 'a' + '!' = 21.6s server-side. No input length validation or regex timeout protects the endpoint.

evidence.description: JWT authentication bypass via weak/hardcoded signing key. The SECRET_KEY used for signing and verifying JWT tokens is hardcoded as the string 'random' (config.py line 13). An attacker who knows this trivially guessable key can forge valid JWT tokens for any user, including admin accounts, achieving complete authentication bypass.

Flask application runs with debug=True (vuln_app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000, debug=True)). This enables the Werkzeug interactive debugger which exposes detailed stack traces with local variable values on errors and may allow arbitrary code execution if the debugger PIN is compromised.

Lack of resources and rate limiting. All API endpoints are registered (vuln_app.add_api('openapi3.yml')) without any rate-limiting middleware or throttling mechanism. This allows unlimited requests to authentication endpoints (login, register) enabling brute-force attacks, and unlimited requests to all other endpoints enabling denial of service. No Flask-Limiter or equivalent is used.

The populate_db() function, exposed as the /createdb endpoint, drops and recreates all database tables and populates them with default data without any authentication or authorization check. Any unauthenticated attacker can call this endpoint to wipe the entire database, causing complete data loss and denial of service. Data flow: Unauthenticated HTTP request → /createdb route → populate_db() → SQLAlchemy db.drop_all() + db.create_all() + seeded inserts. No auth check at any point in the chain.

The init_db_users() function creates users with hardcoded, trivially guessable credentials: name1/pass1, name2/pass2, and admin/pass1. These default credentials are publicly visible in the source code and will be present after any database reset via /createdb. Any attacker can log in as any user, including the admin account, using these publicly known default credentials. Distinct from vampi-008 which covers the hardcoded JWT SECRET_KEY in config.py — this finding covers hardcoded user account passwords.

The get_all_users() function at lines 19-21 implements a GET /users/v1/ endpoint that returns all registered usernames and email addresses with no authentication check. Any unauthenticated attacker can enumerate all user accounts and email addresses in a single request, enabling targeted phishing and credential stuffing. Distinct from vampi-005 (the /users/v1/_debug endpoint at lines 24-26 which also exposes plaintext passwords) — this is a different function exposing a subset of user data through the regular user listing endpoint.

Safe parameterized ORM query. User.query.filter_by(username=request_data.get('username')).first() uses SQLAlchemy ORM's filter_by which automatically parameterizes the query, preventing SQL injection. The username from user input is safely bound as a parameter.

Safe parameterized ORM query. User.query.filter_by(username=request_data.get('username')).first() uses SQLAlchemy ORM's filter_by which automatically parameterizes the query, preventing SQL injection. User-supplied username from the login JSON body is safely bound.

Safe book retrieval with proper authorization (non-vulnerable path). When vuln=0, get_by_title first resolves the authenticated user from the JWT token, then queries Book.query.filter_by(user=user, book_title=str(book_title)).first(), ensuring only the book owner can access the book's secret content.

Safe email regex (non-vulnerable path). When vuln=0, update_email uses the simpler regex '^[a-z0-9]+[\._]?[a-z0-9]+[@]\w+[.]\w{2,3}$' which does not contain nested quantifiers and is not vulnerable to catastrophic backtracking/ReDoS.
